speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Testing now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, just-- Today is December 4th, 1995. The survivor is Anna Goldberg-Schwarcz. Goldenberg-Schwarcz, sorry. G-O-L-D-E-N-B-E-R-G dash S-C-H-W-A-R-C-Z. Maiden name, Trojb, T-R-O-J-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: The interviewer is Marvin Greenberg. We are in West Palm Beach, Florida, USA. The language is English. And this is tape number one. Today is December 4, 1995. The survivor is Anna Goldenberg-Schwarcz. G-O-L-D-E-N-B-E-R-G dash S-C-H-W-A-R-C-Z. We are in West Palm Beach, Florida. The language is English. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Goldenberg-Schwarcz. Could you please tell us where and when you were born? First spell your name, and something about your family and your home town community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good Morning. My name is Ann Goldenberg-Schwarcz. My maiden name was Trojb. I was born in a small town in Poland, Warkowicze. Now it's the Ukraine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived with my paternal grandparents and family in that town, Warkowicze. I was born January 24, 1924. I had a younger brother by the name of Joseph. He was three years younger than I. My parents were young. My father's name was Jacob. My mother's name was Neche. They were wonderful parents. They were in the grain business. Ukraine is known as the breadbasket of Europe, and there was an abundance of grain. And my father and my mother looked into the business, and they saw a future in it. And they embarked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a very small scale and progressed very nicely. They were very successful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very good parents and very good providers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and provided for us the best life that existed in that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived in my paternal grandparents' house with the rest of my father's family, a brother, Moishe, with his family, and an aunt, my father's sister, Hana, with her little girl, Rita. And my grandparents, Abraham and Sara, just like the biblical forefathers and matriarchs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was wonderful to have a family so close and so caring. We as children took advantage of every possible pleasure that was available for us at that time. We did it together with our cousins and with our friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know anything else, because we were very, very sheltered. We were not exposed to the outside world. And life was pretty quiet, uneventful and nice. I just saw around me some other children that weren't so fortunate. Their families were poor, and they could not afford the things that we had, for instance, a bicycle, or ice skating, or even books from school. I went to public school in that shtetl, Warkowicze. I was a good student and conscientious. I took advantage of the library. I loved to read. And less fortunate children that did not have books came to me, and I gladly shared with them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Life was that way 'til I graduated the public school, six years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, I prepared for exams to enter high school. We called it gimnazjum. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very, very prestigious [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a girl from a small town to go to a bigger city and enter a gimnazjum, and I was thankful that my parents provided that possibility for me. That summer, I prepared for the exams. And then I passed the exams in flying colors. And in 1939, my parents brought me to Rowne, and I entered the gimnazjum. It was a wonderful experience for a young girl to-- to see a much broader world. Those were my early teens. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met some wonderful children from different cities. And I was a shy girl, but the atmosphere appealed to me very, very much. And I had a curious mind. I wanted to learn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [NON-ENGLISH] of the gimnazjum was very high. The requirements were big. But I didn't mind. I was studious. We learned languages, too. I learned English, which in my wildest fantasy I couldn't believe that I will ever have to use it as my main language. We learned Latin. I learned Hebrew. Hebrew was a language that I knew already when I entered the gimnazjum. My parents were very traditional, religious people, and they stressed very much my Hebrew education. Being in Warkowicze, in the public school, I took lessons in Hebrew every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And even in the summer, my mother said, to learn Hebrew, there is no vacation. You have vacation from public school, but not from Hebrew. In a way, maybe I resented it because some children had freedom, and they played around. But mother said, and I had to do it. In the long run, I'm very thankful to her that she pressed me to do it, because I have great pleasure knowing that language.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the character of the public school, the constituency? Who all went to that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every child that was six years old entered the public school. There was a mixture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No restrictions, whatsoever. We all attended. And being that we were so young and naive, we didn't see the difference. The only thing that we saw there were some better students and some not so good students. Otherwise, we all felt the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews and Gentiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, Jews and Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the gimnazjum?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gimnazjum was a strictly Jewish gimnazjum [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was accredited from the-- the state and the country, which was a wonderful thing because going to the university, looking far ahead, if the school would not be accredited, then it would be a lot of difficulties to enter a higher learning institution. And this gave us the possibility to look in the future and work towards that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they both co-ed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were co-ed, yes. The public school and the gimnazjum were co-ed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family was religious, and we were very, very traditional. We loved the tradition. Saturday was observed. My father went to shul, to temple. And when he came back, there was a holiday meal. The house looked very nice, different than every other day. And we felt good in that atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship in the town? Were there many Jews? And what was the percentage, and how did they get along with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town, about 2,000 people. Most of them were Jews. The non-Jews lived in the outskirts of the-- of the city. The Jews lived amiable. We had a rabbi, and there were two temples. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most people went, let's say, Saturday to the temple. The older people went every day. I remember my grandfather going every day to shul and pray. But my father went only on Saturday and on the holidays, and so did my mother, on the holidays. The people lived pretty amiable with themselves. I must say that I did not even feel that there is-- there are anti-Semites there. Maybe I was too naive and gullible not to see it, but that's my feeling. We had organizations that provided for the poorer people, for the poorer Jews in our shtetl. And my mother, my father, always contributed generously whoever came and asked for the cause. And we were very Zionistic, my whole family. My father and my mother dreamt of sometimes going to Israel, but their livelihood was there, and we did not-- they, rather, my parents, did not anticipate [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that something terrible can happen to them. So they did not do much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a doctor in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a Jewish doctor, and there was a Gentile doctor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The structure of the-- of the city was like-- like every little town. It-- it had the beauty, the pulse. The lifestyle was what the people made of it. From day to day, most people struggled to make a living, and it was almost uneventful. I do not remember any killing or any scandals. Life was placid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Among us in school, it was also a very pleasant atmosphere. No animosity, just pure rivalry between students.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no crime?

speaker: interviewee
text: No crime whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember, in the house, my parents spoke about the fact that Hitler came to power. They read the book and somehow, they saw that there will be big changes. They did not think that it will affect us directly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but they felt uneasy. Just like Herman [NON-ENGLISH] said, 'the winds were blowing in our direction.' We as children did not feel it. My brother was three years younger, a very playful, very handsome little boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when he came in to the house, he was very, very cheerful and smiling and happy. I was serious, but I did not pay much attention to the political situation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in 1939, after I came back from the gimnazjum after the vacation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first day, when I was supposed to return back for the other term, the war broke out. That was September in 1939. I did not go to Rowne, anymore because the bombs were bombing our little shtetl. And my parents wanted to protect me, and they said, you cannot go now. Let's see what will happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In no time, the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went forward. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I started to be interested in politics, I want to know what's going on and what will happen. I read in the papers that Hitler and Stalin made a pact between themselves, and Poland was divided into two parts. Our part became Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was two weeks that they were fighting, and after that, the Russian army came into our shtetl. It was a Sunday morning. We did not know how to react. Is it good? Is it bad? And, of course, we could not predict the future. We would just sit quietly in the house, waiting for the events to happen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The people among themselves try to figure out, is it better? Is it worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as funny as it may sound, in the shuls, when people went to the synagogues, somehow they were talking about politics. And they felt that it's a much better way for us to be under the Soviet Union rather than under the German occupation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just want to add here that in-- in 1939-- Pardon me. In 1935, my grandfather Abraham died, and my grandmother Sara, with the rest of the family, we remained in the same house. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Under the Soviet occupation, it was very different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And besides the fact that the language changed, I did not see any difference. And when my mother brought me back to gimnazjum, she said that's the only haven. It's a school, like it was before. She didn't worry about the language, because she knew that I'll master that. But at home was tension. For us, maybe more so than for other people, because we were business people and well-to-do. The Soviets called us the 'bourgeois,' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they used to-- the Soviet authorities sent that type of people to Siberia. So my parents were very much concerned that should not happen to us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was a wonderful person. I suppose every child will say it about her father. But when I look back, I see that he had a lot of foresight and a lot of enterprise in him to do things that not the ordinary people would do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the early 30s, my father bought a piece of land. It was a very beautiful, choice piece of land. And he remarked that if he would invest in money, in a bank, the money can devalue it. Or, if he would buy real estate, when there is a war, it can be destroyed, but land will always have its value. As long as the world will exist, the land will be land. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he remarked to my mother, we are sure we'll have dowry for our [NON-ENGLISH]. I was very young then, but he was thinking in terms of the future. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, when the Soviet Union occupied our territory, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were considered also farmers because we owned land. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the Soviet regime took-- took from the private hands. They took the land and made kolkhoz, it's collective farming. And somehow they trusted my father, and my father became the president of the kolkhoz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an unheard of situation, because he was a businessman, a well-to-do. According to the Soviet laws, he wasn't even supposed to be trusted. But the people in the-- in our vicinity that dealt with my father and the business felt that he was a very honorable man, and he is fit for that position. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for almost a year and a half, he was presiding over the kolkhoz. People came to ask him advice, and he gave them very practical answers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a person that felt for people, helped people. And they were-- the authorities and the people were very pleased with him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was in school. There weren't many changes, except that-- that the curriculum, everything was done in the Russian language. In a way, I was pleased to learn the Russian language because it is a beautiful language. And when I was still in public school-- and I loved to read-- I read the Russian classics; Pushkin's, Dostoyevsky's, Tolstoy. I read it in the Polish translation. And now that I acquired the knowledge of the Russian language, I read it in the original, and it was beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is how life was for us 'til June of 1941, when Hitler broke the pact with Russia, and he proceeds in the aggressive pact to go invade it again, Russia. By the way, my brother was home, and he was still much younger, so he went still to public school. They also learned everything in the Russian language. And he also kept on with the Hebrew studies. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the one that was out of town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June 1941 is a date that is, in my memory, the turning point in my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life changed from that day on to such an extent that nobody in their wildest dreams would be able even to-- to fantasize how life can change. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My father, being the president of the kolkhoz, was afraid that when the German army will come to our town, he will be the first victim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we decided to flee, to go to Russia. My mother was always helpful in the business. She was also a very energetic woman. So she said to my father, if you'll be safe, I think I can take care of myself and the children. As long as I know that you will be all right.
